steps.title: Connect exposure and age
steps.content: The team's conclusion is about whether previous language exposure affects a dog's ability to distinguish a familiar language from an unfamiliar one. Since the dogs were raised in similar settings, age can stand in for cumulative exposure: older dogs would generally have spent more time hearing their familiar language than younger dogs.

steps.title: Require the right comparison
steps.content: A compares familiar language with scrambled sounds, but the conclusion is not about speech versus scrambled fragments. B says dogs of all ages respond more to the familiar language, yet it does not show that greater exposure changes the size of the familiar-unfamiliar difference. C compares unfamiliar language with scrambled sounds and could even point away from stronger language discrimination.

steps.title: Use the age pattern
steps.content: The needed finding must compare familiar and unfamiliar language directly and show that the difference is greatest among older dogs. That pattern would connect more prior exposure with a stronger ability to distinguish the two languages. The answer is D.
